transcript:
What you're looking at is five points of light—one dead center, four near the corners—breathing in a red field that's slowly filling in with grain, like static learning to glow.

Here's the thing: the code promises a system eating itself alive. There's a class literally named "RecursiveBirth" with a method called "spawn" that's supposed to branch exponentially—up to four children per node, eight generations deep, which on paper is thousands of elements. But look in the "manifest" function: it's drawing rectangles straight to canvas, not touching the DOM at all. So the "beautiful horror of uncontrolled growth," as the code comments call it, never actually stresses the browser. What you're watching instead is "breathing rate" and "strain" ticking upward every frame, driving a sine-wave pulse that brightens the whole field toward that magenta saturation.

So the piece named "Overflow," built to document its own computational collapse, never collapses—it just breathes harder, forever, on a fixed budget. The failure it's about is quietly absent from the code performing it.

cues.target: Exponential reproduction - the beautiful horror of uncontrolled growth
